items.title: Understanding Heartburn and its Management in GERD
items.content: Heartburn, characterized by a burning sensation behind the sternum, is often linked to gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). It's typically experienced after eating or when lying down. Empirical therapy for GERD is accepted due to this strong association.
keyPoints: Heartburn is the most common esophageal symptom.
Characterized by discomfort behind the sternum, often radiating toward the neck.
Relieved with water or antacids; may interfere with sleep and daily activities.
Strongly associated with GERD, leading to empirical therapy acceptance.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · Diseases of the Esophagus | ESOPHAGEAL STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION | SYMPTOMS OF ESOPHAGEAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Esophagus | ESOPHAGEAL STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION | SYMPTOMS OF ESOPHAGEAL DISEASE
items.title: Differentiating Dysphagia Types and Their Implications
items.content: Esophageal dysphagia can be distinguished by whether it affects solids, liquids, or both. Liquid and solid dysphagia suggests a motility disorder like achalasia, while solid-only dysphagia may indicate structural issues such as strictures or tumors.
keyPoints: Dysphagia described as food 'sticking' in the chest.
Liquid and solid dysphagia suggest motility disorders (e.g., achalasia).
Solid-only dysphagia suggests strictures, webs, rings, or tumors.
Approximately 30% of distal esophageal obstructions are perceived as cervical.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · Diseases of the Esophagus | ESOPHAGEAL STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION | SYMPTOMS OF ESOPHAGEAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Esophagus | ESOPHAGEAL STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION | SYMPTOMS OF ESOPHAGEAL DISEASE
items.title: Identifying Odynophagia and Its Common Causes
items.content: Odynophagia, or pain exacerbated by swallowing, is more common with pill or infectious esophagitis than reflux. In GERD cases, it may indicate an ulcer or extensive erosions.
keyPoints: Odynophagia is pain caused or worsened by swallowing.
More common in pill or infectious esophagitis than in reflux esophagitis.
In GERD, suggests possible ulcers or extensive erosions.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · Diseases of the Esophagus | ESOPHAGEAL STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION | SYMPTOMS OF ESOPHAGEAL DISEASE

items.title: Why Endoscopy is Preferred for Proximal GI Tract Evaluation?
items.content: Endoscopy, particularly esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD), is the most useful test for evaluating the proximal gastrointestinal tract due to its ability to produce high-quality images and perform therapeutic interventions. It offers increased sensitivity for detecting mucosal lesions and abnormalities identifiable by color, such as Barrett's metaplasia or vascular lesions. Endoscopy also allows for biopsy specimen collection and stricture dilation during the examination.
keyPoints: Increased sensitivity for detection of mucosal lesions
Vastly increased sensitivity for color-identifiable abnormalities (e.g., Barrett's metaplasia, vascular lesions)
Ability to obtain biopsy specimens
Ability to dilate strictures during examination
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ENDOSCOPY | RADIOGRAPHY

items.title: When is Radiography Useful in Esophageal Evaluation?
items.content: Radiography, particularly contrast radiography, complements endoscopy by demonstrating conditions such as reflux of contrast media, hiatal hernia, mucosal granularity, erosions, ulcerations, and strictures. It has higher sensitivity for detecting esophageal strictures when using a 13-mm barium tablet. Radiography is also valuable for assessing esophageal function and morphology, tracheoesophageal fistula, altered postsurgical anatomy, extrinsic esophageal compression, hypopharyngeal pathology, and cricopharyngeal muscle disorders.
keyPoints: Sensitivity of radiography for detecting reflux esophagitis ranges from 22 to 95%
Higher sensitivity for detecting strictures with a 13-mm barium tablet
Assesses esophageal function and morphology
Complements endoscopy in tracheoesophageal fistula, altered postsurgical anatomy, extrinsic compression
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ENDOSCOPY | RADIOGRAPHY

items.subtopic: ENDOSCOPIC ULTRASOUND | ESOPHAGEAL MANOMETRY | FUNCTIONAL LUMEN IMAGING PROBE
items.title: Endoscopic Ultrasound for Esophageal Cancer Staging and Evaluation of Barrett's Dysplasia
items.content: Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) is utilized to stage esophageal cancer, evaluate dysplasia in Barrett's esophagus, and assess submucosal lesions due to its high-resolution imaging capabilities.
keyPoints: Combines an endoscope with an ultrasound transducer for transmural imaging.
Provides radial or linear images for guiding fine-needle aspiration.
Major applications include staging esophageal cancer, evaluating Barrett's dysplasia, and assessing submucosal lesions.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ENDOSCOPIC ULTRASOUND | ESOPHAGEAL MANOMETRY | FUNCTIONAL LUMEN IMAGING PROBE

items.subtopic: ENDOSCOPIC ULTRASOUND | ESOPHAGEAL MANOMETRY | FUNCTIONAL LUMEN IMAGING PROBE
items.title: Functional Luminescent Imaging Probe (FLIP) for Esophageal Motility Assessment
items.content: The FLIP catheter measures esophageal cross-sectional area and distensibility during volume-controlled distension, aiding in the evaluation of esophageal motility disorders.
keyPoints: Utilizes high-resolution impedance planimetry along a 16-cm length of the esophagus.
Displays data as diameter topography or real-time cylinder diameter changes.
Correlates well with high-resolution manometry for detecting achalasia and normal contractility.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ENDOSCOPIC ULTRASOUND | ESOPHAGEAL MANOMETRY | FUNCTIONAL LUMEN IMAGING PROBE

items.title: When to Use Reflux Testing in GERD Diagnosis
items.content: Reflux testing is indicated for patients with atypical symptoms or poor response to GERD therapy, especially when endoscopic signs of esophagitis are absent. It helps identify excessive esophageal exposure to gastric fluid by measuring the percentage of time pH <4.
keyPoints: GERD can be diagnosed without endoscopic signs in cases like nonerosive reflux disease.
Reflux testing uses 24- to 96-hour ambulatory esophageal pH recording.
A pH <4 for more than 6% of the day indicates GERD.
Intraluminal impedance monitoring can be added to detect non-acidic reflux events.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · REFLUX TESTING | HIATAL HERNIA

items.title: Understanding Hiatal Hernia Types and Their Clinical Significance
items.content: Hiatal hernias are classified into four types, with type I (sliding) being the most common. Type II and III paraesophageal hernias involve the gastric fundus, while type IV includes other viscera like the colon. Large paraesophageal hernias can lead to complications such as gastric volvulus.
keyPoints: Type I hiatal hernia (sliding) accounts for 95% of cases and is associated with GERD.
Sliding hernias enlarge with increased intraabdominal pressure, swallowing, and respiration.
Type II paraesophageal hernia: gastroesophageal junction remains fixed; gastric fundus herniates.
Type III paraesophageal hernia: combined sliding and paraesophageal hernia.
Type IV hiatal hernia involves other viscera like the colon.
Large paraesophageal hernias can lead to gastric volvulus and require surgical repair.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · REFLUX TESTING | HIATAL HERNIA

items.title: Schatzki Rings: Diagnosis and Management of Esophageal Narrowing
items.content: A Schatzki ring, a type of lower esophageal mucosal ring, is associated with episodic solid food dysphagia when the lumen diameter is less than 13 mm. Typically presenting in individuals over 40 years old, these rings are often acquired and can cause intermittent food impaction known as 'steakhouse syndrome.' Symptomatic Schatzki rings are effectively treated by dilation.
keyPoints: Schatzki ring occurs when lumen diameter is <13 mm
~10–15% of the general population have lower esophageal mucosal rings, usually asymptomatic
Typically presents in individuals older than 40 years
Common cause of intermittent food impaction ('steakhouse syndrome')
Symptomatic rings are treated by dilation
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · RINGS AND WEBS | DIVERTICULA

items.title: Zenker's Diverticulum: Identification and Surgical Treatment
items.content: Zenker's diverticulum, a false diverticulum caused by increased intraluminal pressure due to cricopharyngeal muscle stenosis, occurs in an area of natural weakness known as Killian's triangle. While small Zenker's diverticula are usually asymptomatic, larger ones can cause dysphagia, halitosis, and aspiration. Surgical treatment involves diverticulectomy with cricopharyngeal myotomy or transoral endoscopic myotomy.
keyPoints: Zenker's diverticulum is a false diverticulum
Caused by increased intraluminal pressure from cricopharyngeal muscle stenosis
Occurs in Killian's triangle, an area of natural weakness
Symptoms include dysphagia, halitosis, and aspiration when enlarged
Treated surgically with diverticulectomy and cricopharyngeal myotomy or transoral endoscopic myotomy
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · RINGS AND WEBS | DIVERTICULA

items.title: Esophageal Cancer: Incidence, Types, and Presentation
items.content: Esophageal cancer is less common than colorectal cancer but has a significant mortality rate. The shift from squamous cell carcinoma to adenocarcinoma highlights the importance of recognizing risk factors like reflux disease and Barrett's metaplasia. Adenocarcinoma typically affects the distal esophagus in white males, while squamous cell carcinoma is more common in black males with additional risk factors.
keyPoints: 2023 estimates: ~21,560 new cases (79% men) and 16,120 deaths
Esophageal cancer kills about a third as many patients as colorectal cancer
Shift from squamous cell carcinoma to adenocarcinoma linked to reflux disease
Adenocarcinoma affects distal esophagus in white males; squamous cell carcinoma affects proximal esophagus in black males with smoking, alcohol, caustic injury, and HPV as risk factors
Typical presentation: progressive solid food dysphagia and weight loss
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · TUMORS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.title: Congenital Esophageal Anomalies: Recognition and Complications
items.content: Esophageal atresia is the most common congenital anomaly, occurring in ~2.5 per 10,000 live births. It involves a developmental failure of esophagus fusion with a tracheoesophageal fistula. Surgical correction is typically done early in life, but complications like dysphagia and severe reflux can occur later. Dysphagia lusoria results from vascular anomalies compressing the esophagus.
keyPoints: Esophageal atresia occurs in ~2.5 per 10,000 live births
Commonly involves tracheoesophageal fistula with proximal and distal segment non-fusion
Surgical correction usually within first few days of life
Later complications: dysphagia from anastomotic strictures or absent peristalsis, severe reflux
Dysphagia lusoria caused by aberrant right subclavian artery compressing the esophagus
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · TUMORS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.title: Achalasia Pathogenesis and Presentation
items.content: Achalasia is caused by autoimmune-mediated death of ganglion cells in the esophageal myenteric plexus, often linked to latent human herpes simplex virus 1 infection. It presents with dysphagia for solids and liquids, regurgitation, chest pain, and weight loss.
keyPoints: Population incidence: 1–3 per 100,000
Age of presentation: typically between 25 and 60 years
Classic pathogenesis involves autoimmune-mediated death of ganglion cells
Clinical manifestations include dysphagia for solids and liquids, regurgitation, chest pain, weight loss
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL MOTILITY DISORDERS | ☐ ACHALASIA (part 1)

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Achalasia
items.content: Achalasia must be differentiated from conditions like DES, Chagas' disease, opioid-induced dysmotility, and pseudoachalasia. Pseudoachalasia is more likely with advanced age, abrupt onset (<1 year), and weight loss.
keyPoints: Differential diagnosis includes DES, Chagas' disease, opioid-induced esophageal dysmotility, pseudoachalasia
Pseudoachalasia accounts for up to 5% of suspected cases
Endoscopy is necessary; CT or EUS if high suspicion and endoscopy is nondiagnostic
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL MOTILITY DISORDERS | ☐ ACHALASIA (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Achalasia
items.content: Achalasia diagnosis involves barium swallow x-ray and esophageal manometry. Barium swallow shows a dilated esophagus with poor emptying, air-fluid level, and tapering at the LES. Manometry confirms impaired LES relaxation and absent peristalsis.
keyPoints: Diagnosis by barium swallow x-ray and/or esophageal manometry
Barium swallow features: dilated esophagus, poor emptying, air-fluid level, beak-like taper at LES
Manometry criteria: impaired LES relaxation and absent peristalsis
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL MOTILITY DISORDERS | ☐ ACHALASIA (part 1)

items.title: Therapeutic Options and Outcomes for Achalasia
items.content: Achalasia can be effectively treated with pneumatic dilation or laparoscopic Heller myotomy. Both treatments have similar long-term efficacy, but each has distinct advantages and potential complications.
keyPoints: Pneumatic dilation efficacy: 60-90%
Perforation risk in pneumatic dilation: 0.5-5%
Heller myotomy success rate: 62-90%
Equivalent long-term response (~90%) for both treatments at 5-year follow-up
POEM offers greater symptom relief compared to pneumatic dilation at 2 and 5 years, with similar efficacy to Heller myotomy (>80% symptom relief)
GERD is common post-POEM but manageable with medications
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ☐ ACHALASIA (part 2)

items.title: Complications and Long-term Risks of Untreated Achalasia
items.content: Untreated or inadequately treated achalasia can lead to serious complications, including esophageal stasis esophagitis and an increased risk of squamous cell cancer.
keyPoints: Esophageal dilatation from untreated achalasia predisposes to stasis esophagitis
Association between achalasia and esophageal squamous cell cancer due to prolonged stasis esophagitis
Squamous cell cancer risk increased 17-fold in patients with achalasia compared to controls
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ☐ ACHALASIA (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosing DES: Manometry and Exclusion of Other Esophageal Conditions
items.content: DES is diagnosed primarily via high-resolution manometry, which identifies abnormal esophageal contractions with short latency relative to pharyngeal contraction. Before pursuing a diagnosis of DES, more common causes of chest pain and dysphagia such as cardiac issues, peptic or infectious esophagitis should be excluded through evaluation and/or treatment.
keyPoints: DES is characterized by abnormal contractions with normal LES relaxation.
High-resolution manometry defines DES by short latency contractions in the distal esophagus.
Exclude common causes like cardiac pain, peptic or infectious esophagitis before diagnosing DES.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · DISTAL ESOPHAGEAL SPASM

items.title: Therapeutic Considerations for DES: Indications and Limitations
items.content: Treatment options for DES include nitrates, calcium channel blockers, hydralazine, botulinum toxin, and anxiolytics based on small uncontrolled trials. More invasive procedures like POEM or surgical myotomy are reserved for cases with severe weight loss or intractable pain, which are extremely rare.
keyPoints: Therapeutic options include nitrates, calcium channel blockers, hydralazine, botulinum toxin, and anxiolytics.
POEM or surgical myotomy considered only with severe weight loss or intractable pain.
Indications for invasive procedures are extremely rare.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · DISTAL ESOPHAGEAL SPASM

items.subtopic: NONSPECIFIC MANOMETRIC FINDINGS | GASTROESOPHAGEAL REFLUX DISEASE | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Management of Non-specific Manometric Findings in GERD Patients
items.content: Manometric studies often reveal minor abnormalities that do not diagnose specific esophageal disorders but are associated with common conditions like GERD or cognitive disorders. These findings necessitate a management approach targeting the most prevalent issues, such as GERD or underlying psychiatric conditions.
keyPoints: Minor manometric abnormalities often insufficient for diagnosing achalasia or DES.
Common associations include reflux and psychiatric diagnoses (anxiety, depression).
More than half of these patients exhibit symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome.
Therapy should focus on GERD or cognitive disorders.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · NONSPECIFIC MANOMETRIC FINDINGS | GASTROESOPHAGEAL REFLUX DISEASE | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: NONSPECIFIC MANOMETRIC FINDINGS | GASTROESOPHAGEAL REFLUX DISEASE | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Epidemiology and Clinical Impact of GERD
items.content: GERD is a prevalent condition affecting 10-15% of adults in the U.S., characterized by gastroesophageal reflux leading to various esophageal injuries. The rising incidence of esophageal adenocarcinoma, which increased sixfold over two decades, underscores its clinical significance.
keyPoints: GERD affects an estimated 10-15% of adults in the U.S.
Esophageal injuries range from esophagitis to adenocarcinoma.
9200 incident cases of esophageal adenocarcinoma were noted in 2020, a sixfold increase over 20 years.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · NONSPECIFIC MANOMETRIC FINDINGS | GASTROESOPHAGEAL REFLUX DISEASE | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: NONSPECIFIC MANOMETRIC FINDINGS | GASTROESOPHAGEAL REFLUX DISEASE | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Pathophysiology of GERD and Esophagitis
items.content: Esophagitis in GERD patients results from refluxed gastric acid and pepsin causing inflammation. The pathogenesis involves cytokine-mediated pathways rather than direct injury, with factors like impaired clearance and anatomical changes at the esophagogastric junction exacerbating reflux.
keyPoints: Esophagitis is caused by refluxed gastric acid and pepsin.
Inflammation follows a cytokine-mediated pathway.
Anatomical changes such as hiatal hernia contribute to pathologic reflux.
Factors like abdominal obesity and delayed gastric emptying exacerbate reflux.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · NONSPECIFIC MANOMETRIC FINDINGS | GASTROESOPHAGEAL REFLUX DISEASE | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing GERD and its Extraesophageal Manifestations
items.content: GERD typically presents with heartburn and regurgitation, but can also manifest extraesophageally as chronic cough, laryngitis, asthma, and dental erosions. These associations should be viewed critically, recognizing potential coexistence without causality.
keyPoints: Heartburn and regurgitation are typical symptoms of GERD.
Extraesophageal syndromes include chronic cough, laryngitis, asthma, and dental erosions.
Associations between GERD and other conditions like pulmonary fibrosis or cardiac arrhythmias should be viewed as associations rather than causation.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · SYMPTOMS | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Differentiating GERD from Other Esophageal Conditions
items.content: GERD symptoms must be distinguished from other conditions such as infectious esophagitis, EoE, and coronary artery disease. Endoscopy is crucial for identifying erosive esophagitis at the esophagogastric junction, present in about one-third of GERD patients.
keyPoints: GERD symptoms need differentiation from conditions like infectious esophagitis, EoE, and coronary artery disease.
Endoscopy identifies erosive esophagitis at the esophagogastric junction in about one-third of GERD patients.
Erosive esophagitis is a key endoscopic hallmark of GERD.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · SYMPTOMS | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Barrett's Metaplasia: Risk and Progression to Esophageal Adenocarcinoma
items.content: Barrett's metaplasia, identified by salmon-colored mucosa or intestinal metaplasia histopathologically, significantly increases the risk of developing esophageal adenocarcinoma. Despite potent acid suppression therapies reducing erosive esophagitis and peptic strictures, Barrett's incidence has risen. The progression from Barrett's to adenocarcinoma occurs through low- and high-grade dysplasia stages.
keyPoints: Barrett's metaplasia is associated with a significantly increased risk of esophageal adenocarcinoma.
The rate of cancer development in Barrett's metaplasia is estimated at 0.1–0.3% per year.
Obese white males in their sixth decade are at greatest risk.
Endoscopic screening and surveillance programs' utility for controlling adenocarcinoma risk remains uncertain.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Management of High-Grade Dysplasia in Barrett's Esophagus
items.content: The presence of high-grade dysplasia in Barrett's esophagus necessitates intervention due to a high progression rate to adenocarcinoma and the prevalence of unrecognized coexisting cancer. While historically treated with esophagectomy, which carries 3-10% mortality and significant morbidity, current guidelines recommend endoscopic mucosal ablation therapies as they are effective with substantially less morbidity and no mortality.
keyPoints: High-grade dysplasia in Barrett's mandates further intervention due to high cancer progression risk.
Esophagectomy was historically the standard treatment but has significant risks (3-10% mortality).
Endoscopic mucosal ablation therapy is now recommended for managing high-grade dysplasia with less morbidity and no mortality.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Lifestyle Modifications in GERD Management
items.content: While lifestyle modifications are commonly recommended for managing GERD, their efficacy is generally minimal. However, specific recommendations can benefit subsets of patients based on individual history and symptoms.
keyPoints: Avoidance of refluxogenic foods (e.g., fatty foods, alcohol, spearmint, peppermint, coffee, tea) and acidic foods (e.g., citrus fruits, tomato-based foods).
Adoption of behaviors like head-of-bed elevation and avoiding late meals can help specific symptoms such as nighttime heartburn.
Weight reduction is broadly applicable due to its strong epidemiologic relationship with GERD.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease

items.title: Pharmacological Management of GERD
items.content: The primary pharmacologic approach for GERD involves inhibiting gastric acid secretion, which helps alleviate symptoms and heal esophagitis. The hierarchy of drug effectiveness is PCABs > PPIs > H2RAs.
keyPoints: Potassium competitive acid blockers (PCABs) are more effective than proton pump inhibitors (PPIs), which in turn are more effective than histamine-2 receptor antagonists ($H_{2}RAs$).
Effectiveness is most pronounced with severe esophagitis.
Heartburn relief varies and correlates poorly with the presence or severity of esophagitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease

items.title: Considerations for Long-term GERD Treatment
items.content: Chronic reflux symptoms often necessitate indefinite treatment with PPIs or H2RAs. While side effects are generally minimal, long-term use may affect nutrient absorption and increase susceptibility to infections.
keyPoints: PPIs can cause rare interstitial nephritis, reversible hypomagnesemia, and potentially impair vitamin B12 and iron absorption.
Increased risk of enteric infections like Clostridioides difficile colitis with PPI use.
Observational studies suggest associations between PPI exposure and renal disease, dementia, cardiovascular disease, but evidence is not conclusive.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease

items.title: Diagnosis Criteria for Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE)
items.content: EoE is diagnosed by identifying esophageal symptoms along with histologic evidence of eosinophil-predominant inflammation in mucosal biopsies. The diagnosis should be considered when peripheral blood eosinophilia is present, although its specificity can be problematic due to concomitant atopy.
keyPoints: EoE prevalence: 4–8 cases per 10,000 in the U.S., common in white males aged 30-40.
Diagnosis requires esophageal symptoms and mucosal biopsies showing eosinophil density ≥15 eosinophils per high-power field.
Peripheral blood eosinophilia is seen in 25–50% of patients but lacks specificity due to atopy.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · EOSINOPHILIC ESOPHAGITIS

items.title: Therapeutic Approaches for EoE
items.content: Primary therapy often starts with a proton pump inhibitor (PPI), which is effective in 30–50% of patients. Swallowed topical glucocorticoids are another option, effective in 50–80% of cases. Dupilumab, an IL-4 and IL-13 blocking biologic, is FDA-approved for EoE treatment.
keyPoints: PPIs improve eosinophilic inflammation in 30–50% of patients.
Swallowed topical glucocorticoids are effective in 50–80% of patients.
Dupilumab is the first U.S. FDA-approved biologic for EoE.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · EOSINOPHILIC ESOPHAGITIS

items.title: Complications and Management Strategies in EoE
items.content: Ongoing eosinophilic inflammation can lead to complications such as food impaction, esophageal stricture, and perforation. Esophageal dilation is effective for dysphagia relief but does not address inflammation and carries risks of laceration or perforation.
keyPoints: Complications include food impaction, strictures, and rare perforations.
Esophageal dilation relieves dysphagia in fibrostenosis but is risky due to potential for esophageal laceration/perforation.
Maintenance therapy is necessary due to chronicity and high recurrence rates upon cessation.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · EOSINOPHILIC ESOPHAGITIS

items.title: Recognizing Infectious Esophagitis: Importance of Odynophagia
items.content: Odynophagia is a key symptom that differentiates infectious esophagitis from other causes like reflux esophagitis. Its presence should prompt consideration of an infectious etiology, especially in immunocompromised patients or those with declining CD4 counts.
keyPoints: Odynophagia is characteristic of infectious esophagitis and uncommon with reflux esophagitis.
Infectious esophagitis becomes more common as the CD4 count declines below 100 in AIDS patients.
Symptoms include odynophagia, dysphagia, chest pain, and hemorrhage.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · INFECTIOUS ESOPHAGITIS | CANDIDA ESOPHAGITIS

items.title: Diagnosing and Treating Candida Esophagitis
items.content: Candida esophagitis is diagnosed through endoscopy with biopsy, especially if symptoms persist despite empirical therapy for oral thrush. The characteristic appearance includes white plaques or exudate. Initial treatment involves oral fluconazole, with alternative treatments available for refractory cases.
keyPoints: Empirical therapy for oral thrush may be appropriate; persistent symptoms warrant endoscopy with biopsy.
Characteristic appearance: white plaques or exudate with friability.

Initial treatment: Oral fluconazole (400 mg first day, 200 mg daily) for 7–14 days. Alternative treatments include higher dose fluconazole, voriconazole, itraconazole, posaconazole, intravenous echinocandin, or amphotericin B.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · INFECTIOUS ESOPHAGITIS | CANDIDA ESOPHAGITIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Herpetic Esophagitis
items.content: Herpes simplex virus type 1 or 2 can cause esophagitis, often accompanied by vesicles on the nose and lips. Diagnosis is supported by endoscopic findings of vesicles and superficial ulcerations, with biopsy revealing ground-glass nuclei and Cowdry's type A inclusion bodies. Acyclovir is effective for treatment in immunocompetent patients, while immunocompromised patients require higher doses or alternative antivirals.
keyPoints: Endoscopic findings: vesicles and small superficial ulcerations
Biopsy reveals ground-glass nuclei, Cowdry's type A inclusion bodies, giant cells
Acyclovir dosage for immunocompetent: 200 mg orally five times a day for 7–10 days
Acyclovir dosage for immunocompromised: 400 mg orally five times a day for 14–21 days; alternative treatments include famciclovir (500 mg three times daily) or valacyclovir (1 g three times daily)
Intravenous acyclovir for severe odynophagia: 5 mg/kg every 8 hours for 7–14 days
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · HERPETIC ESOPHAGITIS | CYTOMEGALOVIRUS

items.title: Management of CMV Esophagitis in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: CMV esophagitis primarily affects immunocompromised patients, presenting as large serpiginous ulcers. Diagnosis is confirmed via biopsy showing inclusion bodies or using immunohistology and in situ hybridization tests. Treatment includes ganciclovir or valganciclovir until healing, which may take 3–6 weeks, with maintenance therapy for relapsing cases.
keyPoints: CMV esophagitis occurs mainly in patients with HIV, malignancy, and transplant recipients
Endoscopic appearance: large serpiginous ulcers in distal esophagus
Biopsy from ulcer bases shows pathognomonic inclusion bodies
Ganciclovir dosage for treatment: 5 mg/kg every 12 hours IV
Valganciclovir dosage for treatment: 900 mg orally every 12 hours
Therapy duration until healing may be 3–6 weeks, with possible maintenance therapy
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · HERPETIC ESOPHAGITIS | CYTOMEGALOVIRUS

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Esophageal Perforation
items.content: Esophageal perforation, often due to instrumentation or trauma, presents with pleuritic retrosternal pain, pneumomediastinum, and subcutaneous emphysema. Prompt identification is crucial as mediastinitis can be a severe complication. CT chest is the most sensitive for detecting mediastinal air, while contrast swallow confirms perforation.
keyPoints: Esophageal perforation typically results from instrumentation or trauma at the hypopharynx or gastroesophageal junction.
Symptoms include pleuritic retrosternal pain, pneumomediastinum, and subcutaneous emphysema.
CT chest is most sensitive for detecting mediastinal air; contrast swallow confirms perforation.
Treatment involves nasogastric suction, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and surgical drainage for noncontained leaks.
Early detected contained perforations may be managed conservatively with nasogastric suction, NPO status, and antibiotics.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL PERFORATION | MALLORY-WEISS TEAR

items.title: Understanding Mallory-Weiss Tear and Its Management
items.content: Mallory-Weiss tears result from forceful vomiting, retching, or coughing, causing a nontransmural tear at the gastroesophageal junction. Patients typically present with hematemesis. Most cases resolve spontaneously, but persistent bleeding may require endoscopic intervention.
keyPoints: Mallory-Weiss tears are caused by forceful vomiting, retching, or coughing and occur at the gastroesophageal junction.
Common presentation is hematemesis; antecedent vomiting is often present.
Bleeding usually resolves spontaneously but may require local epinephrine, cauterization, endoscopic clipping, or angiographic embolization if prolonged.
Surgery is rarely needed for Mallory-Weiss tears.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL PERFORATION | MALLORY-WEISS TEAR

items.title: Early Endoscopy for Corrosive Esophagitis
items.content: Corrosive esophageal injury from ingestion of alkali or acid requires prompt endoscopic evaluation to assess and grade mucosal damage, as absence of oral injuries does not exclude esophageal involvement.
keyPoints: Caustic agents can cause severe esophageal injury leading to perforation, bleeding, stricture, or death.
Endoscopy is recommended early to evaluate the extent of mucosal injury.
Glucocorticoids are not beneficial for acute corrosive esophagitis and should be avoided.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · CORROSIVE ESOPHAGITIS | PILL ESOPHAGITIS

items.title: Recognizing Pill Esophagitis
items.content: Pill-induced esophagitis results from a pill lodging in the mid-esophagus, often due to poor pill-taking habits. It is commonly associated with medications like doxycycline and NSAIDs.
keyPoints: Occurs when pills lodge in the mid-esophagus near anatomical structures like the aorta or carina.
Poor pill-taking habits such as inadequate liquid intake or lying down post-ingestion increase risk.
Commonly implicated medications include doxycycline, tetracycline, NSAIDs, and bisphosphonates.
Symptoms typically present as sudden chest pain and odynophagia.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · CORROSIVE ESOPHAGITIS | PILL ESOPHAGITIS

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Esophagus | ESOPHAGEAL STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION | SYMPTOMS OF ESOPHAGEAL DISEASE
items.question: Which symptom is characterized by a discomfort or burning sensation behind the sternum that arises from the epigastrium?
items.explanation: Heartburn is characterized by a discomfort or burning sensation behind the sternum that arises from the epigastrium.

items.question: Which symptom involves the effortless return of food or fluid into the pharynx without nausea?
items.explanation: Regurgitation is the effortless return of food or fluid into the pharynx without nausea.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT an advantage of endoscopy over barium radiography?
options: Increased sensitivity for detection of mucosal lesions.
Higher sensitivity for detecting very proximal esophageal strictures.
Ability to dilate strictures during examination.
items.explanation: Endoscopy has low sensitivity for detecting very proximal esophageal strictures compared to barium radiography.

items.question: What is a major shortcoming of barium radiography?
options: It can demonstrate reflux of contrast media.
It rarely obviates the need for endoscopy.
It has higher sensitivity for detecting esophageal strictures.
items.explanation: Barium radiography seldom eliminates the need for an endoscopic evaluation, which is often required to obtain biopsies or clarify findings.

items.question: For which condition does barium radiography have greater sensitivity than endoscopy?
options: Assessment of esophageal function and morphology.

items.question: Which method can be used to measure the percentage of time that esophageal pH is less than 4?
options: Ambulatory 24- to 96-h esophageal pH recording using a wireless pH-sensitive transmitter or transnasally positioned wire electrode.
items.explanation: Ambulatory 24- to 96-h esophageal pH recording is used to measure the percentage of time that esophageal pH is less than 4, indicating acid reflux.

items.question: What does a value exceeding 6% in esophageal pH monitoring indicate?
items.explanation: A value exceeding 6% indicates GERD, as it reflects excessive esophageal exposure to acid reflux.

items.question: What is the main significance of sliding hiatal hernias?
options: They are primarily associated with gastric volvulus.
They have a high risk of strangulation.
They predispose individuals to developing GERD.
items.explanation: The main significance of sliding hiatal hernias is their propensity to lead to the development of GERD.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · REFLUX TESTING | HIATAL HERNIA

items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of intermittent food impaction, often triggered by meat consumption?
items.explanation: A Schatzki ring is a common cause of intermittent food impaction, known as 'steakhouse syndrome' due to meat being a typical instigator.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · RINGS AND WEBS | DIVERTICULA

items.question: What is the most likely origin of symptomatic cervical esophageal webs in middle-aged women presenting with iron-deficiency anemia?
items.explanation: The combination of symptomatic proximal esophageal webs and iron-deficiency anemia in middle-aged women is known as Plummer-Vinson or Paterson-Kelly syndrome.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · RINGS AND WEBS | DIVERTICULA

items.question: Which type of diverticulum involves herniation through the muscular layer at Killian's triangle?
items.explanation: Zenker's diverticula are false diverticula that occur due to herniation through the muscular layer at Killian's triangle.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · RINGS AND WEBS | DIVERTICULA

items.question: Which type of esophageal cancer is strongly linked to reflux disease and Barrett's metaplasia?
items.explanation: Adenocarcinoma is strongly linked to reflux disease and Barrett's metaplasia.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · TUMORS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.question: What is the most common congenital esophageal anomaly?
items.explanation: Esophageal atresia is the most common congenital esophageal anomaly.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · TUMORS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.question: What condition can cause extrinsic compression of the esophagus due to vascular abnormalities?
items.explanation: Dysphagia lusoria is caused by vascular abnormalities that compress the esophagus.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · TUMORS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature observed on a barium swallow x-ray in patients with achalasia?
options: Dilated esophagus with poor emptying and tapering at the LES giving it a beak-like appearance
Normal esophageal motility with intact peristalsis
Presence of multiple strictures throughout the esophagus
Absence of any structural abnormalities in the esophagus
items.explanation: A barium swallow x-ray in achalasia typically shows a dilated esophagus with poor emptying and tapering at the LES, giving it a beak-like appearance.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL MOTILITY DISORDERS | ☐ ACHALASIA (part 1)

items.question: What is the most sensitive diagnostic test for achalasia?
items.explanation: Esophageal manometry is the most sensitive diagnostic test for achalasia as it identifies early disease before esophageal dilatation and food retention.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL MOTILITY DISORDERS | ☐ ACHALASIA (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical clinical manifestation of achalasia?
items.explanation: While some patients may complain of heartburn, it is paradoxical and not a typical manifestation; dysphagia, regurgitation, and chest pain are more common.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL MOTILITY DISORDERS | ☐ ACHALASIA (part 1)

items.question: Which treatment option for achalasia involves injecting botulinum toxin into the LES?
items.explanation: Botulinum toxin is used in pharmacologic therapy to inhibit acetylcholine release from nerve endings, improving dysphagia.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL MOTILITY DISORDERS | ☐ ACHALASIA (part 1)

items.question: Which therapy for achalasia has been shown in a European randomized controlled trial to have an equivalent response rate of ~90% at 5-year follow-up when compared to laparoscopic Heller myotomy?
items.explanation: A European randomized controlled trial demonstrated an equivalent response rate of ~90% for both pneumatic dilation and laparoscopic Heller myotomy at 5-year follow-up.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ☐ ACHALASIA (part 2)

items.question: What is the major complication associated with pneumatic dilation in achalasia treatment?
items.explanation: The major complication of pneumatic dilation is perforation, with a reported incidence of 0.5–5%.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ☐ ACHALASIA (part 2)

items.question: Which procedure involves creating a tunnel in the submucosa of the esophageal wall to transect the circular muscle of the LES?
items.explanation: The POEM technique involves creating a tunnel in the submucosa of the esophageal wall to transect the circular muscle of the LES.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ☐ ACHALASIA (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic radiographic finding of distal esophageal spasm (DES)?
items.explanation: A 'corkscrew esophagus' is a characteristic radiographic finding of DES.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · DISTAL ESOPHAGEAL SPASM

items.question: Which manometric feature helps define distal esophageal spasm (DES) with high-resolution manometry?
options: occurrence of contractions in the distal esophagus with short latency relative to pharyngeal contraction
uncoordinated activity in the proximal esophagus
high-amplitude and prolonged contractions without regard to timing
items.explanation: High-resolution manometry defines DES by contractions with short latency relative to pharyngeal contraction.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · DISTAL ESOPHAGEAL SPASM

items.question: What should be the primary consideration when evaluating chest pain that mimics angina pectoris?
items.explanation: Cardiac pain must be the primary consideration when evaluating chest pain that mimics angina pectoris.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · DISTAL ESOPHAGEAL SPASM

items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as exacerbating gastroesophageal reflux?

items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · NONSPECIFIC MANOMETRIC FINDINGS | GASTROESOPHAGEAL REFLUX DISEASE | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: What is the primary cause of esophagitis in GERD patients?
options: Direct caustic injury by gastric acid
items.explanation: Esophagitis in GERD patients is primarily caused by a cytokine-mediated inflammatory pathway rather than direct caustic injury.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · NONSPECIFIC MANOMETRIC FINDINGS | GASTROESOPHAGEAL REFLUX DISEASE | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: Which symptom is considered less common in GERD?
items.explanation: Dysphagia and chest pain are somewhat less common symptoms of GERD compared to heartburn and regurgitation.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · SYMPTOMS | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is a potential mechanism for extraesophageal manifestations of GERD?
options: Direct contact between refluxate and supraesophageal structures
Vagal reflex causing gastric acid secretion
items.explanation: Extraesophageal manifestations can occur via regurgitation with direct contact between the refluxate and supraesophageal structures.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · SYMPTOMS | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition must be considered early in differential diagnosis due to its potentially lethal implications?
items.explanation: Coronary artery disease must be considered early in the differential diagnosis due to its potentially lethal implications.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · SYMPTOMS | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which complication of GERD is associated with an increased risk of esophageal adenocarcinoma?
items.explanation: Barrett's metaplasia is associated with a significantly increased risk for the development of esophageal adenocarcinoma.

items.question: What is the estimated annual rate of cancer development in patients with Barrett's metaplasia?
items.explanation: The rate of cancer development in Barrett's metaplasia is estimated at 0.1–0.3% per year.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which demographic group is at the greatest risk for developing esophageal adenocarcinoma from Barrett's metaplasia?
options: Obese white males in their sixth decade of life
Non-obese Asian females in their third decade of life
Obese African American males in their fourth decade of life
Non-obese Caucasian females in their fifth decade of life
items.explanation: The group at greatest risk is obese white males in their sixth decade of life.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which lifestyle modification is most broadly applicable for managing GERD?
options: Avoidance of fatty foods and alcohol.
Elevation of the head of the bed.
Adoption of behaviors to minimize reflux.
items.explanation: The passage states that weight reduction is the most broadly applicable recommendation for managing GERD.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease

items.question: What is the dominant pharmacologic approach to GERD management?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the dominant pharmacologic approach to GERD management is with inhibitors of gastric acid secretion.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease

items.question: Which class of drugs is more efficacious than proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) for healing esophagitis?
items.explanation: The passage states that potassium competitive acid blockers (PCABs) are more efficacious than proton pump inhibitors for healing esophagitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease

items.question: What is a common management strategy for chronic reflux symptoms?
options: Intermittent use of proton pump inhibitors.
Indefinite treatment with PPIs or H2RAs as necessary for symptom control.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that a common management strategy is indefinite treatment with PPIs or H2RAs as necessary for symptom control.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease

items.question: Which genetic loci have been identified as susceptibility elements in eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE)?
items.explanation: Genome-wide analysis studies demonstrated susceptibility elements at 5q22 (thymic stromal lymphopoietin) and 2p23 (CAPN14) in EoE.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · EOSINOPHILIC ESOPHAGITIS

items.question: What is the peak density of eosinophils per high-power field required for histologic confirmation of EoE?
items.explanation: Histologic confirmation is made with the demonstration of esophageal mucosal eosinophilia (peak density ≥ 15 eosinophils per high-power field).
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · EOSINOPHILIC ESOPHAGITIS

items.question: Which therapy has been approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration for EoE?
items.explanation: The first U.S. Food and Drug Administration-approved treatment for EoE is the interleukin 4 and 13-blocking biologic dupilumab.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · EOSINOPHILIC ESOPHAGITIS

items.question: What percentage of patients with EoE show improvement in eosinophilic inflammation when treated with a proton pump inhibitor (PPI)?
items.explanation: Primary therapy often starts with a PPI, which is effective at improving eosinophilic inflammation in 30–50% of patients.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · EOSINOPHILIC ESOPHAGITIS

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is a characteristic symptom of infectious esophagitis?
items.explanation: Odynophagia is a characteristic symptom of infectious esophagitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · INFECTIOUS ESOPHAGITIS | CANDIDA ESOPHAGITIS

items.question: In which group of patients does Candida esophagitis become more common?
options: AIDS patients with low CD4 counts
items.explanation: Candida esophagitis becomes more common in AIDS patients as the CD4 count declines.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · INFECTIOUS ESOPHAGITIS | CANDIDA ESOPHAGITIS

items.question: What is the preferred initial treatment for Candida esophagitis?
items.explanation: The preferred initial treatment for Candida esophagitis is oral fluconazole.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · INFECTIOUS ESOPHAGITIS | CANDIDA ESOPHAGITIS

items.question: Which virus is associated with esophagitis characterized by vesicles and small, superficial ulcerations?
items.explanation: Herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2 are associated with esophagitis characterized by vesicles and small, superficial ulcerations.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · HERPETIC ESOPHAGITIS | CYTOMEGALOVIRUS

items.question: In immunocompetent hosts with herpes simplex esophagitis, what is the typical duration of acyclovir treatment?
items.explanation: Acyclovir is typically used for 7–10 days in immunocompetent hosts with herpes simplex esophagitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · HERPETIC ESOPHAGITIS | CYTOMEGALOVIRUS

items.question: Which diagnostic method can be helpful to identify acyclovir-resistant strains of herpes simplex virus?
items.explanation: Culture or polymerase chain reaction (PCR) assays are helpful to identify acyclovir-resistant strains of herpes simplex virus.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · HERPETIC ESOPHAGITIS | CYTOMEGALOVIRUS

items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of upper gastrointestinal bleeding due to nontransmural tears?
items.explanation: Mallory-Weiss tears are caused by vomiting, retching, or vigorous coughing.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL PERFORATION | MALLORY-WEISS TEAR

items.question: What is the most sensitive imaging modality for detecting mediastinal air in esophageal perforation?
items.explanation: CT of the chest is most sensitive for detecting mediastinal air in esophageal perforation.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL PERFORATION | MALLORY-WEISS TEAR

items.question: What treatment is rarely needed for a Mallory-Weiss tear?
items.explanation: Surgery is rarely needed for a Mallory-Weiss tear.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL PERFORATION | MALLORY-WEISS TEAR

items.question: Which of the following is NOT recommended for improving clinical outcomes in acute corrosive esophagitis?
options: Assessment and grading of injury to the esophageal mucosa
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids have not been shown to improve clinical outcomes in acute corrosive esophagitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · CORROSIVE ESOPHAGITIS | PILL ESOPHAGITIS

items.question: What is the most common location for a pill to lodge, causing pill-induced esophagitis?
options: Upper esophagus near the cricoid cartilage
Lower esophagus near the gastroesophageal junction
Mid-esophagus near the crossing of the aorta or carina
items.explanation: The most common location for pill lodging is in the mid-esophagus near the crossing of the aorta or carina.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · CORROSIVE ESOPHAGITIS | PILL ESOPHAGITIS

items.question: Which medication is NOT commonly implicated in causing pill esophagitis?
items.explanation: Aspirin is not listed as a common cause of pill esophagitis in the passage.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · CORROSIVE ESOPHAGITIS | PILL ESOPHAGITIS

items.statement: Type I hiatal hernia accounts for approximately 95% of all hiatal hernias.
items.explanation: Type I, or sliding hiatal hernia, comprises about 95% of the overall total.

items.statement: Intraluminal impedance monitoring can only detect acidic reflux events.
items.explanation: Intraluminal impedance monitoring detects reflux events irrespective of whether they are acidic.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · REFLUX TESTING | HIATAL HERNIA

items.statement: Epiphrenic diverticula are often associated with esophageal hypercontractile disorders.
items.explanation: Epiphrenic diverticula can be associated with achalasia or other esophageal hypercontractile disorders.

items.statement: Schatzki rings are typically congenital.

items.statement: Benign esophageal tumors are commonly symptomatic and often lead to early detection.
items.explanation: Benign esophageal tumors are uncommon and usually discovered incidentally.

items.statement: Pharmacologic therapies are the most effective long-term treatment for achalasia.

items.statement: GERD is uncommon after POEM and usually requires surgical intervention.

items.statement: Distal esophageal spasm (DES) is more common than achalasia.

items.statement: Endoscopy can be useful to identify alternative structural and inflammatory lesions that may cause chest pain in patients suspected of having DES.

items.statement: Endoscopic mucosal ablation therapy for high-grade dysplasia has a mortality rate ranging from 3 to 10%.

items.statement: Peripheral blood eosinophilia is a highly specific finding for diagnosing EoE.

items.statement: Empiric elimination diets followed by systematic reintroduction have been effective for both children and adults with EoE.
items.explanation: Empiric elimination of common food allergies followed by systematic reintroduction has been an effective diet therapy in both children and adults.

items.statement: Odynophagia is uncommon with reflux esophagitis.
items.explanation: Odynophagia should raise suspicion of an alternative etiology, such as infectious esophagitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · INFECTIOUS ESOPHAGITIS | CANDIDA ESOPHAGITIS

items.statement: Candida esophagitis is typically treated with intravenous echinocandin as the first line of therapy.
items.explanation: The preferred initial treatment for Candida esophagitis is oral fluconazole.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · INFECTIOUS ESOPHAGITIS | CANDIDA ESOPHAGITIS

items.statement: CMV esophagitis is commonly seen in immunocompetent individuals.
items.explanation: CMV esophagitis primarily occurs in immunocompromised patients.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · HERPETIC ESOPHAGITIS | CYTOMEGALOVIRUS

items.statement: Ganciclovir is used for the treatment of CMV esophagitis.
items.explanation: Ganciclovir is an effective treatment option for CMV esophagitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · HERPETIC ESOPHAGITIS | CYTOMEGALOVIRUS

items.statement: Radiation esophagitis can lead to chronic strictures years after exposure.
items.explanation: Submucosal fibrosis and degenerative tissue changes from radiation esophagitis may cause stricturing years later.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL PERFORATION | MALLORY-WEISS TEAR

items.statement: Mallory-Weiss tears are typically treated with immediate surgery.
items.explanation: Surgery is rarely needed for Mallory-Weiss tears; they often resolve spontaneously or with less invasive treatments.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL PERFORATION | MALLORY-WEISS TEAR

items.statement: Pill-induced esophagitis can be prevented by taking pills with adequate liquid.
items.explanation: Poor pill-taking habits, such as inadequate liquid intake, contribute to pill esophagitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · CORROSIVE ESOPHAGITIS | PILL ESOPHAGITIS

items.statement: Glucocorticoids are recommended for the treatment of acute corrosive esophagitis.
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids have not been shown to improve clinical outcomes in acute corrosive esophagitis and are not recommended.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · CORROSIVE ESOPHAGITIS | PILL ESOPHAGITIS

items.assertion: Esophageal dysphagia for both liquids and solids suggests a motility disorder.
items.reason: Achalasia is an example of a motility disorder that can cause dysphagia for both liquids and solids.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Globus sensation is often due to an esophageal obstruction.
items.reason: Globus sensation is frequently relieved by swallowing and is associated with anxiety or obsessive-compulsive disorders.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; globus sensation is not typically due to an esophageal obstruction.

items.assertion: FLIP can be used to evaluate patients with esophageal dysphagia.
items.reason: There is excellent correlation between FLIP panometry and high-resolution manometry in the detection of achalasia and normal contractility.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why FLIP can be used to evaluate esophageal dysphagia.

items.assertion: Achalasia can be differentiated into subtypes based on manometric patterns.
items.reason: High-resolution manometry allows differentiation of achalasia into three subtypes based on pressurization patterns in the nonperistaltic esophagus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Pseudoachalasia is more common in younger patients.
items.reason: Pseudoachalasia accounts for up to 5% of suspected achalasia cases and is associated with advanced age, abrupt onset of symptoms, and weight loss.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; pseudoachalasia is more likely in older patients. The reason is true.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · ESOPHAGEAL MOTILITY DISORDERS | ☐ ACHALASIA (part 1)

items.assertion: The rising incidence of esophageal adenocarcinoma parallels the increasing incidence of GERD.
items.reason: Pathologic reflux is a consequence of the interplay between progressive anatomic distortion and physiology.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain the assertion about adenocarcinoma.

items.assertion: Pepsin, bile, and pancreatic enzymes can injure the esophageal epithelium.
items.reason: Their noxious properties are dependent on acidity for activation.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains that their harmful effects depend on an acidic environment.

items.assertion: Visceral hypersensitivity is increasingly recognized as a cofactor in GERD symptom genesis.
items.reason: Multiple potential mechanisms for symptom genesis operate beyond mucosal erosion and activation of afferent sensory nerves.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why visceral hypersensitivity is recognized as a cofactor.

items.assertion: The perceived frequency and severity of heartburn correlate well with the presence or severity of esophagitis.
items.reason: Paradoxically, the perceived frequency and severity of heartburn correlate poorly with the presence or severity of esophagitis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the reason correctly states that there is a poor correlation between heartburn perception and esophagitis.

items.assertion: Weight reduction has no benefit with respect to reflux.
items.reason: The strong epidemiologic relationship between body mass index and GERD supports the recommendation for weight reduction.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because, although the benefit with respect to reflux cannot be assured, there is a strong epidemiologic relationship supporting weight reduction.

items.assertion: Eosinophilic esophagitis is primarily diagnosed through endoscopic findings.
items.reason: Characteristic endoscopic esophageal findings include loss of vascular markings, multiple rings, furrows, whitish exudate, and strictures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, diagnosis also requires histologic confirmation with eosinophil-predominant inflammation.

items.assertion: Systemic glucocorticoids are recommended for treating EoE.
items.reason: They provide significant benefits beyond those achieved with topical glucocorticoids.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; systemic glucocorticoids are not generally recommended due to side effects and lack of proven benefit beyond that achieved with topical glucocorticoids.
items.reference: Diseases of the Esophagus · EOSINOPHILIC ESOPHAGITIS

items.assertion: Candida esophagitis has a characteristic appearance of white plaques or exudate with friability.
items.reason: Oral thrush is present, and empirical therapy is appropriate.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the presence of oral thrush leading to empirical therapy does not explain the characteristic appearance.

items.assertion: Severe corrosive injury to the esophagus may lead to stricture formation.
items.reason: Healing of more severe grades of caustic injury is commonly associated with severe stricture formation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
